coredata.dc:title: A novel one-step immunoassay using mesoporous core-shell Pd@Pt nanoparticles as an alternative to horseradish peroxidase for amantadine detection
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Xiong, Jincheng
Sun, Boyan
Zhang, Shuai
Qin, Linqian
Zhang, Jiangjiang
Jiang, Haiyang
coredata.dc:description: The inherent limitations of natural enzymes hinder their stable application under harsh conditions, highlighting the urgent need for a stable alternative signal tracer. Herein, core-shell palladium@platinum (Pd@Pt) nanoparticles (NPs) with mesoporous and dendritic nanostructures were synthesized using a modified ultrasound-assisted chemical reduction method, resulting in a 25 % improvement in relative activity compared to previously reported methods. The yielding nanoparticles exhibited enhanced peroxidase-like activity with specific activity of 54.4 U/mg, and their Michaelis constant and maximum reaction velocity were 6.2 and 48 timers higher, respectively, than those of horseradish peroxidase (HRP). The Pd@Pt NPs were conjugated with antibodies via electrostatic adsorption, demonstrating their potential as a viable alternative to HRP. The rough surface structure of the Pd@Pt NPs not only enhanced the efficiency of antibody immobilization to approximately 90 %, but also, through a pH-regulated antibody immobilization strategy, facilitated the orientation of antibody recognition regions, thereby improving inhibition efficiency. A one-step immunoassay based on the Pd@Pt NPs immunoprobe was then developed, with amantadine (AMD) serving as a proof of concept. The proposed method showed a 2.8-fold increase in sensitivity, a 400-fold expansion in the linear range, and a 1-h reduction in detection time compared to the traditional “gold standard” ELISA. The practical applicability of the proposed method was validated in real chicken and pork samples, with coefficients of variation all less than 17.49 %, and a strong correlation with commercial kits was observed. These results suggest that Pd@Pt NPs are a promising signal-generating probe with significant potential as an alternative to HRP, offering improvements in sensitivity, linear range, stability, and other key performance characteristics.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bimetallic nanozymes
Peroxidase-like activity
Horseradish peroxidase
One-step immunoassay
Amantadine

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of Pd@Pt NPs and its immuoprobes
Peroxidase-like activities assessment of Pd@Pt NPs
Stability of Pd@Pt NPs and HRP
Evaluation of the antibody immobilization and binding efficiency of Pd@Pt immunoprobe
Synthesis and characterization of Pd@Pt NPs
Preparation and characterization of the Pd@Pt NPs immunoprobe
Performances of one-step immunoassay for AMD detection
Determination of AMD in chicken and pork samples

The authors would like to thank Dr. Wenchong Shan (National Nanfan Research Institute, Chinese Academy of Agricultural Sciences, Sanya China) for his assistance with graphic design and drawing.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation and characterization of Pd@Pt NPs. (a) Schematic illustration of synthesis route. (b) UV–Vis monitors the dynamic changes of before and after reaction; insert image is under natural light. (c) SEM image. (d) TEM image. (e) Hydrodynamic diameter distribution. (f) High-resolution TEM image of individual particle with crystal lattice plane (right). (g) EDX spectroscopy. (h) XPS spectrum. (i) High-resolution XPS spectrum of Pt4f.

Peroxidase-like activity evaluation of Pd@Pt NPs and HRP. UV–Vis spectrum of Pd@Pt NPs and HRP catalyzed reaction at 650 nm (a) 450 nm (b). (c) Concentration-dependent reaction time curves. (d) Reaction time curves of Pd@Pt NPs and HRP within 6 min. (e) Zoom initial reaction velocities of (d). The specific activity of Pd@Pt NPs (f) and HRP (g). Michaelis-Menten curve of Pd@Pt NPs (h) and HRP (i).

Pd@Pt NPs immunoprobe characterization. UV–Vis spectrum (a), hydrodynamic diameter (b) and Zeta potential (c) of Pd@Pt NPs and Pd@Pt-mAb. (d) The catalytic activity of Pd@Pt NPs before and after conjugation. (e) Regression curve of OD450 against anti-AMD concentrations. (f) Immobilization efficiency of Pd@Pt NPs under difference mAb concentrations. (g) Binding efficiency of Pd@Pt-mAb with different coupling pH. (h) Schematic illustration of antibody number and direction distribution on the surface of Pd@Pt NPs at different coupling pH.

Analytical performances of one-step immunoassay. (a) Schematic illustration of one-step immunoassay for AMD detection. (b) The color trend of nanozymes and HRP-based ELISA. The calibration curve of OD450 against the logarithm concentrations of Pd@Pt NPs (c), Au@Pt NPs (d) and HRP-based ELISA (e); Insert image is quantitatively linear curve. (f) Performance comparison of immunoassay based on three tracers. (g) Specific analysis of one-step immunoassay. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Real samples analysis. (a) Sample pretreatment protocol for AMD detection. (b) Elimination of matrix effect of chicken (b) and pork (c). (d) Precision and accuracy. (e) Correlation analysis between one-step immunoassay and commercial products.
Comparison of this work with our previously published work for AMD detection.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: ALP, Alkaline phosphatase; CDs, Carbon dots; WS2, Tungsten disulfide; MBs, Magnetic beads; FQIA, Fluorescence quenching immunoassay; LFIA, Latera flow immunoassay; IFE-IA. Inner filter effect-based immunoassay; FRET-IA, Fluorescence resonance energy transfer-based immunoassay.

head.ce:title: A novel one-step immunoassay using mesoporous core-shell Pd@Pt nanoparticles as an alternative to horseradish peroxidase for amantadine detection

College of Veterinary Medicine
China Agricultural University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The inherent limitations of natural enzymes hinder their stable application under harsh conditions, highlighting the urgent need for a stable alternative signal tracer. Herein, core-shell palladium@platinum (Pd@Pt) nanoparticles (NPs) with mesoporous and dendritic nanostructures were synthesized using a modified ultrasound-assisted chemical reduction method, resulting in a 25 % improvement in relative activity compared to previously reported methods. The yielding nanoparticles exhibited enhanced peroxidase-like activity with specific activity of 54.4 U/mg, and their Michaelis constant and maximum reaction velocity were 6.2 and 48 timers higher, respectively, than those of horseradish peroxidase (HRP). The Pd@Pt NPs were conjugated with antibodies via electrostatic adsorption, demonstrating their potential as a viable alternative to HRP. The rough surface structure of the Pd@Pt NPs not only enhanced the efficiency of antibody immobilization to approximately 90 %, but also, through a pH-regulated antibody immobilization strategy, facilitated the orientation of antibody recognition regions, thereby improving inhibition efficiency. A one-step immunoassay based on the Pd@Pt NPs immunoprobe was then developed, with amantadine (AMD) serving as a proof of concept. The proposed method showed a 2.8-fold increase in sensitivity, a 400-fold expansion in the linear range, and a 1-h reduction in detection time compared to the traditional “gold standard” ELISA. The practical applicability of the proposed method was validated in real chicken and pork samples, with coefficients of variation all less than 17.49 %, and a strong correlation with commercial kits was observed. These results suggest that Pd@Pt NPs are a promising signal-generating probe with significant potential as an alternative to HRP, offering improvements in sensitivity, linear range, stability, and other key performance characteristics.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Amantadine (AMD) is a typical antiviral drug with a tricyclic amine structure. It effectively inhibits viral activity by preventing viral uncoating and fusion, and it has been approved for the clinical prevention and treatment of viral infections [1,2]. However, due to its antiviral and growth-promoting properties, AMD has been illegally used in the poultry industry, leading to significant issues such as drug resistance, drug residues, public health risks, and environmental contamination [3,4]. In view of these negative impacts on human health and the environment, many countries, including China, have enacted regulations that explicitly prohibit the use of AMD in poultry farming [5–7]. The relevant instrument analysis methods have been announced for AMD monitoring, such as gas chromatography-mass spectrometry [8] and liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry [9]. However, these methods are inevitably subject to sophisticated instruments, complex pretreatment processes, professional operators and demanding experimental conditions, which limit their practicality for rapid AMD detection. Therefore, development of a simple, efficient and sensitive method is essential for AMD monitoring.

Enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) based on highly specific recognition between antibody and antigen is a powerful analytical tool with the merits of simplicity, easy operation, high throughput, and favorable sensitivity, which has been extensively applied in food safety, in vitro diagnostics and environmental monitoring [10–12]. In routine AMD detection, typical ELISA involves the formation of an antigen-antibody complex followed by the introduction of an enzyme-labeled antibody to trigger a chromogenic reaction, which results in signal variation but prolongs the detection time. For instance, our team has previously developed several immunoassays for the quantitative detection of AMD in poultry based on magnetic-assisted biotinylated single-chain variable fragment antibody [13], inner filter effect [14] and fluorescence resonance energy transfer [15]. However, these methods typically require around 2 h, which limits their suitability for rapid on-site screening. Additionally, conventional ELISAs rely on natural enzymes such as horseradish peroxidase (HRP) or alkaline phosphatase as signal tracers to catalyze the conversion of colorless substrates into colored products, enabling the sensitive detection of trace targets [16]. However, natural enzymes inherently have some drawbacks, such as high cost, harsh transport and storage conditions, and incline to lose their catalytic activity due to heat-, pH-, or chemical-induced denaturation [17]. In addition, the enzyme-antibody conjugate usually connects the enzyme and the antibody through a covalent linkage with complicated preparation and purification procedure, this process will inevitably decrease the catalytic activity of the enzyme and capture efficiency of antibody bound to antigen, thereby reducing sensitivity of ELISAs [18,19]. Consequently, considerable efforts have been made to develop novel enzyme mimics that offer low cost, high stability, and simplified surface conjugation to replace natural enzymes and expand the practical applications of ELISAs.

Nanozymes, a class of nanomaterials that exhibit natural enzyme-like activities, have attracted significant attention from researchers worldwide due to their distinct advantages, such as superior catalytic activity, ease of preparation and purification, cost-effectiveness, convenient storage, and high stability [20,21]. Integrating nanozymes with ELISA has markedly enhanced detection performance by improving sensitivity, accuracy, stability, and operational efficiency [22,23]. In this regard, a variety of nanozymes have been developed to enhance immunoassays, with bimetallic platinum (Pt)-based nanozymes receiving the most attention [24]. Pd@Pt NPs, a typical mesoporous core−shell bimetallic nanozyme, are particularly effective in this respect [25]. The incorporation of the Pd core alters the surface energy of the Pt shell, regulates the electronic structure of the active sites, optimizes and increases the number of active Pt sites, and significantly enhances catalytic activity [26]. The mesoporous structure provides a high specific surface area, facilitating contact with substrate molecules and promoting the coupling and high loading of biorecognition elements, thus further improving catalytic efficiency [27]. Additionally, the Pd core stabilizes the structure, preventing aggregation and dissolution of Pt, significantly enhancing its long-term stability under harsh conditions. Furthermore, the Pd core reduces the amount of Pt required while maintaining catalytic activity, which is highly economical for large-scale applications. While most research on Pd@Pt NPs has focused on macromolecule detection [28–30], their application in small-molecule competitive immunoassays remains limited. The challenges include (i) limited antigenic epitopes of small molecules leading to low recognition efficiency, (ii) non-directional conjugation during antibody functionalization, and (iii) high background signal caused by nonspecific adsorption. These factors compromise the efficiency, sensitivity, and accuracy of small-molecule detection.

In response to the pressing challenges outlined above, this work seeks to address these issues while exploring the potential of Pd@Pt NPs as a novel signal amplification tracer to replace HRP. Core-shell Pd@Pt NPs with mesoporous and dendritic nanostructures were synthesized using a modified ultrasound-assisted chemical reduction method, which significantly enhanced their enzyme activity and catalytic efficiency. Then proposed a pH-regulated antibody immobilization strategy to control the orientation of antibody recognition regions, and to strike a balance between binding and inhibition efficiency. Subsequently, a one-step competitive immunoassay based on Pd@Pt NPs immunoprobe was developed, with AMD serving as the proof of concept. The proposed immunoassay demonstrated enhanced detection sensitivity, a broader linear range, reduced detection time, and minimized non-specific adsorption, highlighting its superior performance in small-molecule analysis. This approach is easily adaptable to other small-molecule contaminants and presents strong potential for Pd@Pt NPs to replace HRP in small-molecule immunoassays.

K2PtCl4, Na2PdCl4, HAuCl4·3H2O, H2PtCl6·6H2O, Pluronic F127, horseradish peroxidase (160 U/mg), ascorbic acid, and bovine serum albumin (BSA) were procured from Sigma-Aldrich Company (St. Louis, MO, USA). HRP-labeled anti-mouse IgG was obtained from Jackson Immuno-Research, Inc (West Grove, NJ, USA). Skimmed milk powder was purchased from Solarbio Bioscience & Technology Co., Ltd (Beijing, China). High binding 96-well microplate (300–400 ng IgG/cm2) used for immunoassay was supplied by Yunpeng Technology Co., Ltd (Xiamen, China). High-precision pH test paper (6.5 < pH < 10.0) was provided by Merck Technology Co., Ltd (Darmstadt, Germany). The 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine, H2O2 (30 %), acetic acid, sodium acetate, potassium carbonate (K2CO3), hydrochloric acid (36 %), sulfuric acid (98 %) and other reagents used in this study were sourced from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Shanghai Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China) with analytical grade. The anti-AMD mAb and AMD-BSA were produced and preserved in our lab [4]. Standards (99.0 % purity) of this work were purchased from National Institutes for Food and Drug Control (Beijing, China). The goat anti-mouse IgG and commercial AMD ELISA kits were kindly provided by Beijing WDWK Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Blank chicken and pork samples were purchased as meat from local markets in Beijing.
The instruments used for nanomaterials characterization were listed in the supporting information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Pd@Pt NPs and its immuoprobes
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis procedure of Pd@Pt NPs was performed by slightly modifying the method in Ref. [25]. The procedure is as follows: 3.6 mL of K2PtCl4 (20 mM), 0.4 mL of Na2PdCl4 (20 mM), and 44 μL of hydrochloric acid (6 M) were mixed. To this solution, 40 mg of Pluronic F127 was added, followed by sonication until a clear orange-yellow solution was obtained. Subsequently, 4 mL of 100 mM ascorbic acid was rapidly added, and the solution was placed in an ultrasonic cleaner for 15 min at 35 °C (40 W). After sonication, the mixture was transferred to a water bath magnetic stirrer and stirred at 30 °C for 24 h, during which the solution color changed from brown to dark black. The resulting precipitate was collected by centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 10 min and washed five times with an acetone-water mixture (v/v: 50:50). The final product was re-dissolved in 8 mL of ultrapure water at a concentration of 1.25 mg/mL and stored at 4 °C for future use.

The Pd@Pt NPs immunoprobe was prepared using the electrostatic adsorption method as per our previous report [31–34]. In a 1.5 mL tube, 125 μL of Pd@Pt NPs was mixed with 875 μL of deionized water. The pH of the solution was adjusted to 8.3 using 0.1 M K2CO3 solution. Then, 2 μL of anti-AMD mAb was added, and the mixture was incubated on a 3D rotating mixer (85 rpm/min) for 1 h. Following this, 100 μL of 10 % BSA solution was added to block any excess binding sites and incubated for an additional 30 min to prevent nonspecific adsorption. The solution was then centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min, the supernatant discarded, and the conjugate resuspended in 1 mL of PBS buffer (containing 1 % BSA) for washing. Finally, the precipitate was resuspended in 125 μL of storage buffer (0.01 M PBS containing 5 % sucrose, 2.5 % BSA, and 0.5 % Tween-20) and stored at 4 °C until use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Peroxidase-like activities assessment of Pd@Pt NPs
ce:section.ce:para:
The peroxidase activity of the enzymes was determined based on previously published literature [35]. A steady-state kinetic method was used to measure the peroxidase activity of Pd@Pt NPs and HRP, using TMB as the model substrate. The measurements were performed in a 96-well plate with 0.1 M NaAc-HAc buffer (pH 4.0). Briefly, Pd@Pt NPs (0.1 μg) or HRP (0.05 μg) were added to the NaAc-HAc buffer, followed by the addition of TMB substrate. The TMB concentrations ranged from 0.05 to 2 mM for Pd@Pt NPs and from 0.5 to 4 mM for HRP. Subsequently, H2O2 was added to a final concentration of 100 mM to initiate the reaction. The absorbance of oxidized TMB (ox-TMB) at 650 nm was measured every 10 s for 1 min. The rate of absorbance increase was then converted into the catalytic reaction velocity and fitted to the substrate concentration using nonlinear regression based on the Michaelis-Menten equation:
V =
V max
×
[ S ]
/
(
K m
+
[ S ]
)
Key parameters, such as the Michaelis constant (Km) and maximum reaction velocity (Vmax), were calculated.

The same method was used for specific activity (SA) determination. The enzyme concentration was varied within the dynamic range: 0.025–0.5 μg/mL for Pd@Pt NPs and 2.5–50 ng/mL for HRP. Nanozyme activity (units) was calculated using the following equation: UV/(ε × l) × (△A/△t). where V represents the total reaction volume, ε the molar absorption coefficient, l the path length of light, and ΔA/Δt the catalytic reaction velocity. After calculating U, the specific activity (SA) was determined using the formula
SA = U / m
where m represents the mass of Pd@Pt NPs or HRP. SA reflects the enzyme's ability to catalyze reactions under specific conditions and is a critical indicator of enzyme efficiency.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability of Pd@Pt NPs and HRP
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the stability differences between nanozymes and biological enzymes, the effects of various factors such as temperature (20–80 °C), pH (4–12), saline concentration (0–2 M), and storage duration (0–30 days) on Pd@Pt NPs (100 ng/mL) and HRP (10 ng/mL) were evaluated. Both enzymes were subjected to the specified conditions for 2 h, after which the absorbance at 650 nm was measured before and after treatment. The relative activity of each enzyme was then calculated, serving as an indicator of its stability under the tested conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the antibody immobilization and binding efficiency of Pd@Pt immunoprobe
ce:section.ce:para: The standard curve of OD450 versus antibody concentration was established to evaluate antibody immobilization efficiency. In brief, varying concentrations of anti-AMD mAb (0.01–1 μg/mL) were used for coating, followed by the addition of an equal volume of HRP-labeled anti-mouse IgG for immunoreaction. Subsequently, TMB substrate was added to trigger the color reaction, which was then halted to record OD450 values using a multimode reader. For the preparation of Pd@Pt NPs immunoprobes, a series of anti-AMD mAb concentrations ranging from 2 to 20 μg were used. The solutions were centrifuged, and the supernatant was collected, diluted 100-fold for further detection, and the corresponding concentrations were calculated using the established standard curve. The antibody immobilization efficiency was expressed as follows: Immobilization efficiency = [(total antibody) – (supernatant antibody∗100)]/(total antibody) × 100 %

A paired antibody binding assay was conducted to analyze antibody binding efficiency. Goat anti-mouse IgG was coated onto a 96-well microplate. Various volumes of K2CO3 were added to Pd@Pt NPs to adjust the pH (6.8–9.0) and coupled with an equal mass of anti-AMD mAb to prepare the Pd@Pt NPs immunoprobe. The Pd@Pt NPs immunoprobe with different coupling pH values was diluted and added to the microplate for immunoreaction. After adding TMB substrate to initiate the color reaction and stopping the reaction, OD450 values were recorded using a multimode reader. The intensity of OD450 is directly proportional to the binding efficiency of the immunoprobe, reflecting the distribution of antibodies on the surface of Pd@Pt NPs at different coupling pH values.

The coating antigen (AMD-BSA) was diluted 5000-fold using carbonate buffer (pH = 9.6) and added to a 96-well high-binding transparent ELISA plate, followed by incubation at 4 °C for 12 h. After the coating process, the plates were washed once with washing buffer (0.01 M PBS containing 0.05 % Tween-20) and then blocked by adding 1 % skimmed milk (150 μL per well), followed by incubation at 37 °C for 1.5 h. After discarding the blocking solution, a series of 25 μL of AMD standard solution (or sample solution) and 25 μL of Pd@Pt NPs immunoprobe (15 μg/mL) were sequentially added to each well and incubated at 37 °C for 40 min. Following five consecutive washing steps, 100 μL of mixed TMB substrate, rather than HRP-labeled anti-mouse IgG, was added to each well for direct color reaction. After incubation at 37 °C for 20 min, 50 μL of stopping solution (2 M H2SO4) was added to terminate the reaction, and the OD450 values were measured and recorded using a multifunctional reader. The standard curve was fitted using linear regression: y = ax + b, with the logarithm of AMD concentration as the x variable and the OD450 value as the y variable to plot the immunoreaction's standard curve.

The purchased chicken and pork samples were confirmed to be free of AMD through LC-MS/MS analysis. The sample pretreatment protocol followed was similar to previous methods developed in our lab [4,14]. In brief, the chicken and pork samples were first crushed and homogenized, after which 2 ± 0.02 g of the homogenized sample were weighed and placed in a centrifuge tube. Next, 2.0 mL of 80 % acetonitrile (v/v, containing 1 % acetic acid) was added to the tube and mixed thoroughly for 5 min. The mixture was then centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min, and the supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane. The filtrate was subsequently diluted tenfold with PBS buffer prior to detection (Fig. 5a). Each sample was tested in triplicate to ensure reliability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of Pd@Pt NPs
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis process of mesoporous core-shell Pd@Pt NPs is illustrated in Fig. 1 a. Pd2+ and Pt2+ served as the metal sources, while F127 acted as a stabilizing agent. Upon the addition of ascorbic acid (AA), Pd NPs formed the core first, with F127 adsorbing Pt2+, which gradually deposited on the surface of the Pd core to form dense catalytic sites, eventually developing into a mesoporous core-shell dendritic structure. In comparison to the method reported by Ataee-Esfahani et al. [25], our study employed a modified ultrasound-assisted chemical reduction technique. This method does not require strict control of temperature or ultrasonic power, avoiding the excessively rapid reduction of Pt that could occur with direct ultrasonic reduction, which often results in large particle sizes and poor distribution of Pt atoms on the Pd core, leading to suboptimal catalytic activity. As shown in Fig. S1, our modified method improved the relative activity by 25 % compared to the reported method, likely due to the slow and controlled deposition of Pt on the Pd core, which allows for full exposure of catalytic active sites.

The dynamic changes in the reaction were observed using UV–Vis spectroscopy, as shown in Fig. 1b. The characteristic peaks of the metal precursors at 280 and 400 nm disappeared, and the solution color changed from brown to deep black as the reaction reached completion. Fig. 1c and d shows the morphology of Pd@Pt NPs, revealing that the nanoparticles are uniformly distributed and have consistent sizes ranging from 50 to 60 nm. The average size, calculated from a sample of 100 nanoparticles, was 56 nm. Uniform mesopores were observed on the surface of the nanostructures, and their spherical morphology resembled Chinese hydrangeas. The hydrodynamic diameter of the Pd@Pt NPs followed a normal distribution, measured at 60.6 nm with a polydispersity index (PDI) of 0.19, indicating good dispersibility and colloidal stability (Fig. 1e). High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) characterization of individual particles, shown in Fig. 1f, revealed numerous dendritic branch structures extending from the Pd core. The inset of Fig. 1f demonstrates that the nanoparticles are highly crystalline, with lattice fringes coherently extending across the particles. These fringes, spaced 0.23 nm apart, were randomly distributed on the particle surface. The rough and uneven edges of the nanoparticles, along with the large number of catalytically active sites hidden among unsaturated atoms, likely contribute to their high catalytic activity. The energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectrum (Fig. 1g) indicates the elemental composition of Pd@Pt NPs, with Pd and Pt at 8.7 % and 91.3 % by weight, and 14.9 % and 85.1 % by atomic percentage, respectively, demonstrating the high distribution of Pt. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used to determine the valence states of the Pd@Pt NPs. The characteristic peaks of Pd3d (∼341 and ∼345 eV), C1s (∼285 eV), and Pt4f (∼71 and ∼75 eV) were observed in the corresponding positions (Fig. 1h). Further analysis using high-resolution XPS (HRXPS) of the Pt4f region (Fig. 1i) revealed peaks corresponding to Pt0+4f and Pt2+4f. The Pt0+4f7/2 and Pt0+4f5/2 peaks were centered at 71.3 and 74.6 eV, respectively, while the Pt2+4f7/2 and Pt2+4f5/2 peaks were centered at 72.2 and 75.7 eV, respectively, indicating the mixed valence states of Pt in the particles. According to the fitting results, Pt0+ accounted for approximately 56.8 % of the total Pt area, suggesting that a significant portion of Pt atoms are located on the particle surface, highly exposed to the reaction environment, thus accelerating electron transfer and significantly enhancing catalytic activity.

The catalytic activity of an enzyme is a critical parameter in evaluating the function of a nanozyme. Therefore, the catalytic activity of Pd@Pt NPs was assessed through a peroxidase-triggered TMB oxidation reaction in the presence of H2O2, with HRP used as a reference enzyme. As shown in Fig. 2 a, both TMB and H2O2 solutions were colorless. Pd@Pt NPs could catalyze TMB to form a light blue color without H2O2, whereas HRP could not, indicating that Pd@Pt NPs possess intrinsic oxidase-like activity. Upon the addition of H2O2, the solution turned dark blue, accompanied by a prominent absorption peak at 650 nm, demonstrating that Pd@Pt NPs also exhibit strong peroxidase-like activity. The addition of 2 M H2SO4 terminated the reaction and amplified the signal, increasing absorbance by 1.4–3.8 times (Fig. 2b). As shown in Fig. 2c, the reaction rate increased with higher concentrations of Pd@Pt NPs, with 50 μg/mL of Pd@Pt NPs reaching a steady state within 1 min, displaying a clear concentration-dependent behavior. The time-dependent absorption spectra (Fig. 2d) indicated that Pd@Pt NPs oxidized TMB faster than HRP within 6 min. By focusing on the initial linear portion of the reaction time curve (Fig. 2e), the reaction rate of Pd@Pt NPs was found to be four times higher than that of HRP. The specific activity (SA, U/mg), used to quantitatively compare the catalytic efficiency of different enzymes, is essential for evaluating enzymatic performance. As shown in Fig. 2f, the SA of Pd@Pt NPs for catalyzing the TMB chromogenic reaction was calculated to be 54.4 U/mg, surpassing most reported nanozymes (Table S1). The SA for HRP was calculated to be 138.2 U/mg (Fig. 2g), consistent with the value indicated on the product label.

The initial reaction velocities were measured under varying TMB concentrations, while keeping the H2O2 concentration constant. The relationship between initial reaction velocities and substrate concentrations was then used to establish the Michaelis-Menten curve (Fig. 2h and i). Each curve was well-fitted using the Lineweaver-Burk model, and a double reciprocal equation was applied to calculate the enzyme kinetics, including the Michaelis constant (Km) and the maximum reaction rate (Vmax) (inset of Fig. 2h and i). The results showed that the affinity of Pd@Pt NPs for TMB (Km = 0.081 mM) was superior to that of HRP (Km = 0.499 mM), demonstrating a 6.2-fold increase. Additionally, the Vmax of Pd@Pt NPs for TMB (Vmax = 1 × 10−7 Ms−1) was significantly higher than that of HRP (Vmax = 4.8 × 10−8 Ms−1), reaching 48 times the maximum reaction rate, indicating a substantially higher turnover velocity in converting substrates into oxidized products compared to HRP.

The stability of enzymes is crucial for their practical applications. As shown in Fig. S2a, the relative activity of Pd@Pt NPs decreased significantly at pH > 11, whereas the activity of HRP sharply declined at both pH < 6 or pH > 11. Pd@Pt NPs remained stable within the temperature range of 25–80 °C, while HRP became unstable and nearly lost all activity above 37 °C (Fig. S2b). At a salt ion concentration of 0.5 M, the relative activity of Pd@Pt NPs retained 57.6 % of their initial activity, whereas HRP was almost completely inactivated, with both enzymes losing activity at higher salt concentrations (2 M) (Fig. S2c). After 30 days of storage at room temperature, Pd@Pt NPs retained 96.6 % of their relative activity, while HRP lost its activity (Fig. S2d). These findings demonstrate that Pd@Pt NPs possess excellent stability across a range of pH levels, temperatures, and storage conditions, as well as a degree of salt tolerance. This superior stability suggests that Pd@Pt NPs have significant potential as viable alternatives to HRP in various biotechnological applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of the Pd@Pt NPs immunoprobe
ce:section.ce:para: Pd@Pt NPs were conjugated with anti-AMD mAb via an electrostatic adsorption method without additional modifications. The immunoprobe was characterized using UV–Vis spectroscopy, zeta potential measurements, and hydrodynamic diameter analysis. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the characteristic absorption peak of the protein at 280 nm was observed after conjugation. The hydrodynamic diameter increased from 60.6 nm to 108.5 nm, while the PDI remained relatively unchanged, indicating that the immunoprobe maintained good dispersibility (Fig. 3b). The zeta potential of the Pd@Pt NPs immunoprobe shifted significantly from −33.6 mV to −19.2 mV (Fig. 3c), confirming that the antibody was successfully bound to the Pd@Pt NPs via electrostatic adsorption. Furthermore, the catalytic activity of Pd@Pt NPs before and after conjugation was assessed, and the relative activity of the Pd@Pt immunoprobe at 1 μg/mL retained 80 % of its initial level, with no significant change observed at 0.1 μg/mL (Fig. 3d). These results demonstrate that conjugation does not adversely affect the intrinsic catalytic activity of the nanozymes. The Pd@Pt immunoprobe integrates both enzyme signal amplification and specific recognition, providing a robust bifunctional probe and laying the groundwork for the development of a one-step dual-mode immunoassay.

Antibody immobilization and binding efficiency are critical factors for enhancing the performance of immunoassays. A competitive ELISA was employed to establish a regression curve between antibody concentration and absorbance intensity, evaluating the antibody immobilization efficiency (Fig. 3e). The results demonstrated that across an antibody addition range of 2–20 μg, the immobilization efficiency of Pd@Pt NPs remained consistently around 90 %, indicating that the Pd@Pt NPs possess an excellent capacity for mAb loading (Fig. 3f). Additionally, the antibody binding efficiency and orientation significantly impact the performance of competitive immunoassays. Goat anti-mouse antibodies were directly coated on microplates, and Pd@Pt immunoprobes prepared at different coupling pH values were used to evaluate their binding performance. Fig. 3g shows that binding efficiency exhibited an initial increase followed by a decline, peaking at a coupling pH of 7.4. This suggests that the number of antibodies distributed on the Pd@Pt surface is the largest at this pH value. The results of the competition inhibition test at various coupling pH values (Fig. S2d) showed that as the coupling pH increased, the absorbance of negative samples decreased, while the inhibition rate rose.

The observed asynchrony between binding and inhibition efficiency suggests that the quantity and orientation of antibody on the nanoparticle surface is regulated by pH. We hypothesize that the number and orientation of antibodies on the Pd@Pt NPs surface vary with different coupling pH values, as illustrated in Fig. 3h. The antigen binding region (Fab) and constant region (Fc) of antibody exhibit distinct charge characteristics due to the varying acidic and alkaline amino acid compositions [36]. The isoelectric point (pI) of the mAb is generally 8.2, when the coupling pH is below the pI of mAb, the positively charged antibodies are adsorbed onto the nanoparticle surface via electrostatic interactions, leading to high binding efficiency but low inhibition. This suggests that the Fab region preferentially inserts into the nanoparticle surface, compared to the Fc region. At a pH near the pI, neutral antibodies are adsorbed onto the nanoparticle surface via hydrophobic interactions, resulting in moderate binding and inhibition rates. In this case, the Fab region extends outward, while the Fc region becomes embedded in the nanoparticle surface. When the pH exceeds the pI of the mAb (up to pI + 0.5), negatively charged antibodies are adsorbed via covalent bonding with alkaline amino acids such as lysine, arginine, and histidine. The repulsion between the Fc region and the nanoparticle surface promotes the outward extension of the Fab region, enhancing antigen recognition, but reducing binding efficiency. However, at significantly higher pH levels, strong charge repulsion between the antibodies and the nanoparticles renders the antibodies more hydrophilic. This results in a substantial reduction in antibody conjugation due to inhibited electrostatic and hydrophobic interactions, with absorbance intensity and inhibition rate no longer showing further changes. The objective of this study is to investigate how varying coupling pH can control both the quantity and orientation of antibodies on the Pd@Pt NP surface, to strike a balance between the preservation of molecular recognition properties and optimal immunoassay performance. This pH-regulated antibody immobilization strategy is straightforward to operate and cost-effective, offering potential as a template for the application of nanozymes in various immunoassays.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performances of one-step immunoassay for AMD detection
ce:section.ce:para: Benefiting from the efficient catalytic signal amplification of nanozymes and the specific recognition capability of antibodies, the Pd@Pt immunoprobe was employed as an advanced bifunctional tag to establish a one-step immunoassay targeting AMD due to its potential threat to food safety and public health. As illustrated in Fig. 4 a, the Pd@Pt NPs-based one-step immunoassay enables rapid sample result readout without the need for conjugating primary and secondary antibodies, unlike conventional ELISA. The assay principle is as follows: the antigen is immobilized on a 96-well high-absorption transparent microplate, after which AMD and Pd@Pt immunoprobes are sequentially added, allowing competitive binding to the antigen to form an antigen-antibody complex. The highly efficient catalytic activity of the Pd@Pt immunoprobe subsequently triggers the TMB/H2O2 color development system. The immobilization efficiency of the Pd@Pt NPs immunoprobe is inversely proportional to AMD concentration, meaning the absorbance at OD450 nm decreases as the AMD concentration increases. Systematic exploration of critical parameters is essential to optimize the performance of the one-step immunoassay. A series of conditions were optimized, and the optimal assay parameters are as follows: antigen concentration (6K dilution of AMD-BSA), blocking buffer type (1 % skimmed milk), blocking buffer concentration (1 %), coupling pH (pH 8.3), buffer pH (pH 7.4), antibody concentration (7.5 μg), probe concentration (15 μg/mL), and incubation time (40 min). Detailed information on parameter optimization can be found in the supporting information (Fig. S3). As shown in Fig. 4b, the color intensity decreases as AMD concentration increases, and the visual detection range of nanozyme-ELISA (using Pd@Pt NPs and Au@Pt NPs) is broader compared to HRP-ELISA.

Regression curves of OD450 versus AMD concentration were established for Pd@Pt NPs (Fig. 4c), Au@Pt NPs (Fig. 4d), and HRP (Fig. 4e). The Pd@Pt NPs and Au@Pt NPs-based ELISAs exhibited excellent linear ranges of 0.05–200 ng/mL and 0.1–500 ng/mL, respectively, with limits of detection (LOD) calculated to be 0.018 ng/mL for Pd@Pt NPs and 0.036 ng/mL for Au@Pt NPs. In contrast, the linear range and LOD for HRP-ELISA were 0.1–10 ng/mL and 0.051 ng/mL, respectively. A comparative analysis of the LOD and linear ranges for the three tracers is presented in Fig. 4f. The LOD of Pd@Pt NPs showed a 2- and 2.83-fold enhancement compared to Au@Pt NPs and HRP, respectively. Notably, the linear range of Pd@Pt NPs and Au@Pt NPs-based ELISAs was 400 and 500 times wider than that of HRP. These results demonstrate that nanozyme-ELISA not only achieves superior sensitivity but also effectively broadens the detection range, making it highly beneficial for detecting small molecule chemical contaminants present in a wide concentration range in food samples.

Ensuring the specificity of an immunoassay is crucial for its practical application. The specificity of the one-step immunoassay was evaluated using several antiviral drugs and antibiotics, including amantadine (AMD), rimantadine (RMD), somantadine (SMD), memantine, acyclovir, ribavirin, enrofloxacin, ofloxacin, sulfamethazine, tetracycline, florfenicol, and chloramphenicol (the chemical structures are shown in Fig. S4). The concentration of all standards was set at 10 ng/mL. As shown in Fig. 4g, only the addition of AMD, RMD, and SMD suppressed the signal intensity. The high response from RMD and SMD likely originated from their structural similarity to AMD, with their cross-reactivity (CR) calculated at 269 % and 514 %, respectively (Fig. S5), which aligns with previous reports [37]. Given that RMD and SMD are rarely used in the livestock industry, their actual risks are negligible. Additionally, none of the other tested compounds induced significant suppression of absorbance intensity. These results confirm the superior specificity of the proposed one-step immunoassay for AMD detection.

This work was compared with previously reported methods for AMD detection developed by our team in terms of signal tags, methods, samples, sensitivity, linear range and detection time, and results are listed in Table 1 . Differentiated from previous work, this method employs a completely novel signal tag, enabling rapid readout in a single-step incubation. As a result, the detection time is significantly reduced compared to ELISA. Furthermore, this approach achieves the highest sensitivity recorded in our team's research, thanks to the precise control of pH, which allows for the directional distribution of antibodies, further enhancing the detection sensitivity for AMD. Notably, the linear range of this method is much broader than that of previous immunoassays, enabling the screening of AMD across a wide concentration range without additional more complicated operations. These exciting findings offer a powerful new regulatory tool for food safety detection technology.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of AMD in chicken and pork samples
ce:section.ce:para: Inspired by the excellent performance of the one-step immunoassay based on the Pd@Pt bifunctional immunoprobe, its practical applicability was evaluated by detecting AMD in pork and chicken samples. The sample pretreatment protocol for AMD extraction is shown in Fig. 5a. Considering the matrix effect in real samples, the extract solution was diluted multiple times to assess its purification status. After a 10-fold dilution, the linear curves overlapped with those obtained in buffer, indicating that the matrix effect was effectively eliminated (Fig. 5b and c). The intra-assay recoveries in chicken and pork samples ranged from 90.95 % to 123.50 % and from 69.92 % to 86.44 %, respectively, with coefficients of variation (CV) below 6.72 %. The inter-assay recoveries ranged from 95.04 % to 105.95 % for chicken and from 66.30 % to 84.35 % for pork, with CV values between 6.18 % and 17.49 % (Fig. 5d). To further validate the method, 15 chicken samples with varying concentrations were analyzed using both the proposed one-step immunoassay and a commercial kit. As shown in Fig. 5e, the two methods achieved a high correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.95, and the regression equation was fitted as y = 0.81x+0.046, demonstrating good consistency between the one-step immunoassay and commercial kits. These results indicate that the one-step immunoassay based on Pd@Pt NPs exhibits satisfactory accuracy and precision in complex real samples, highlighting its potential as a novel enzyme signal tracer to replace HRP in the field of food safety.

In summary, bimetallic Pd@Pt NPs were successfully synthesized using an ultrasound-assisted one-pot chemical reduction method. These mesoporous core-shell structured nanoparticles displayed uniform morphology, excellent environmental stability, superior catalytic activity, and high antibody immobilization efficiency. Through electrostatic adsorption, Pd@Pt NPs were coupled with antibodies, yielding a bifunctional immunoprobe with enhanced catalytic activity and specific antigen recognition. Precise control over the coupling pH allowed for optimization of antibody density and orientation on the nanoparticle surface, facilitating efficient antigen capture and amplifying the detection signal. This approach presents a straightforward and versatile strategy for enhancing immunoassay performance. A one-step immunoassay based on the Pd@Pt NPs immunoprobe was developed, and AMD was used as a proof of concept. The assay exhibited a 2.8-fold increase in sensitivity, a 400-fold expansion in linear range, and a reduction in assay time by over 1 h compared to the conventional ELISA “gold standard.” The method's practicality was further confirmed through real sample analysis. This work elucidates the how to balance preservation of molecular recognition properties and efficient antigen capture via pH-regulated antibody immobilization strategy, providing valuable insights into the interaction mechanisms between noble metal nanozymes and biorecognition elements. Given the scalability and versatility of noble metal nanozymes, bimetallic nanozymes hold significant potential to replace HRP in next-generation point-of-care testing platforms and can be further combined with other functional sensing elements to expand their application to environmental monitoring, in vitro diagnostics, and beyond.

Jincheng Xiong: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Data curation, Conceptualization. Boyan Sun: Validation, Methodology. Shuai Zhang: Writing – review & editing. Linqian Qin: Methodology. Jiangjiang Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Resources. Haiyang Jiang: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank Natural Science of Foundation of China (32373096, 22104049), the Postdoctoral Fellowship Program (Grade B) of CPSF (GZB20240291), the China Post-doctoral Science Foundation (2024M761309), and Beijing Institute of Technology Research Fund Program for Young Scholars. The authors would like to thank Dr. Wenchong Shan (National Nanfan Research Institute, Chinese Academy of Agricultural Sciences, Sanya China) for his assistance with graphic design and drawing.
